rationale: Containers can be scheduled, but timing is usually secondary.
Container requests strongly correlate with OS, runtime, and process isolation.
Some container asks include setup or package tooling steps.
Documents are not the primary signal for container routing.
Containers often appear in service and deployment integrations.
Plain chat is usually not a container request.
Skills may run on schedules, but are primarily reusable workflows.
Some skills touch system tasks, but that is not the main cue.
Named skills and reusable workflows map strongly to tooling.
Document handling often benefits from reusable skill execution.
Skills sometimes orchestrate external APIs or services.
Simple chat requests usually do not imply a skill.
Recurring timing is the strongest signal for cron-style execution.
Cron can monitor system tasks but timing remains dominant.
Cron may trigger tools, though tooling is not the primary signal.
Document requests alone do not strongly indicate cron.
Scheduled polling and health checks often target integrations.
Direct chat answers rarely imply persistent scheduling.
MCP can be scheduled indirectly, but timing is usually weak.
MCP is not mainly about local OS-level execution.
MCP can expose tools, but the strongest cue is external integration.
Documents may be accessed over MCP, though not as the main signal.
External servers, APIs, and remote tool hosts strongly indicate MCP.
Simple direct answers are usually not MCP calls.
Direct answers usually avoid recurring timing or persistence.
Simple responses rarely require system-level execution.
Direct chat generally does not imply reusable tooling.
Some direct requests are simple document explanations or summaries.
An integration may be discussed directly without execution.
Plain explanatory chat strongly maps to direct answering.
